rulings.Case Title: SCHAPIRO-THORN INC., VS. DANIEL L. MITCHELL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Plaintiff, Cross-Defendant Schapiro-Thorn, Inc., For An Order To Dismiss The Cross-Complaint Of Defendant, Cross-Complainant Daniel L. Mitchell, Or Other Appropriate Relief
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 1, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF/CROSS-DEFENDANT SCHAPIRO-THORN INC.'S Motion for Order To Dismiss The Cross-Complaint Of Defendant/Cross-Complainant Daniel L. Mitchell, Or Other Appropriate Relief. Granted. The Court will enter an amended judgment. The Arbitration Award adjudicated Mitchell's claims against Shapiro-Thorn. (Declaration of William Henley, Ex. H, Arbitration Award, p. 3, "Mr. Mitchell did not appear at the hearing and pursuant to JAMS Rule 31b is deemed to have waived any affirmative claims that he may have had against [Shapiro-Thorn]. Regardless, the Arbitrators deemed the claim denied.") Therefore, judgment should be entered in favor of Shapiro-Thorn on the cross-complaint. Shapiro-Thorn shall prepare an amended judgment that is identical to the original judgment, but includes the language: "Judgment is entered in favor of Shapiro-Thorn on the cross-complaint." Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: EQUITARE ASSOCIATES, INC VS. DANIEL HARVEY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And Notice Of Motion For Summary Judgment And Summary Adjudication Of Issues
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, May 1, 2015, Line 5, DEFENDANTS DANIEL HARVEY, AND CUSHMAN & WAKEFIELD OF CALIFORNIA, INC'S Motion For Summary Judgment And Summary Adjudication Of Issues. The motion for summary judgment is denied. The Court grants and denies the motion for summary adjudication in part. The Court grants summary adjudication in favor of Mr. Harvey as to cause of action 1 (breach of contract) and cause of action 4 (goods and services rendered) because Plaintiff admits that Mr. Harvey was acting as the employee and agent of Cushman & Wakefield ("C&W"). (Undisputed Fact 1). The general rule is that the principal (C&W) is responsible for any contractual obligation. See Cal. Civil Code sec. 2337. The court grants summary adjudication on cause of action 3 (money had and received) in favor of Mr. Harvey and C&W as unopposed. The court denies summary adjudication as to Mr. Harvey and C&W on cause of action 2 (intentional interference with prospective business advantage) because Defendants failed to maintain their initial burden of production. Defendants merely rely on Plaintiff's Complaint and the declaration of Mr. Harvey to maintain that burden. Mr. Harvey could be liable for this tort if the trier of fact determines that his conduct was wrongful. See Cal. Civil Code sec. 2343. Defendants' motion does not sufficiently address Plaintiff's theory of liability based on Plaintiff's relationship with Heroku, Salesforce's purchase of Heroku, Heroku's occupancy of 600 Townsend St. and how defendants induced plaintiff to part with valuable information regarding the property and Heroku with the promise of a commission. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: RAMON AGUAS et al VS. DULCICH INC. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ramon Aguas, Eulia Aguas Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday May 1, 2015, Line 4, Ramon Aguas, Eulia Aguas' Motion To Compel Further Discovery Responses. Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff Ramon Aguas ("Aguas") Motion to Compel Further Responses to Requests for Admissions, Deemed Matters Admitted, and for Monetary Sanctions against Defendant Dulcich, Inc. dba Pacific Seafood ("Dulcich") is GRANTED. Sanctions against Dulcich and in favor of Plaintiff is GRANTED in an amount to be determined. Aguas shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK HOOSHMAND VS. KATHARINA WOHL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday May 1, 2015, Line 5, PLAINTIFF MARK HOOSHMAND'S Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl (part 1 of 3). Pro Tem Judge Douglas Robbins, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff First Mercury Insurance Co.'s ("First Mercury") Motion for Terminating Sanctions, for Attorneys' Fees, or for Alternative Sanctions against Defendant the Law Offices of Bornstein & Bornstein ("Bornstein"), is GRANTED in PART and DENIED in PART. Monetary sanctions against Bornstein and in favor of First Mercury is GRANTED in the amount of $650. First Mercury shall prepare a form of the order consistent with this tentative ruling and shall bring a copy of the proposed order to the hearing, if any. In the absence of a hearing, the prevailing party shall timely comply with Cal. R. Ct. 3.1312. This Motion presents a strange technical conundrum. Here, in January 2015, First Mercury won three Orders as against Bornstein, one of which is relevant here: the Order to Compel Answers to Interrogatories and Response to Demand for Inspection of Documents and for Sanctions, Jan. 12, 2015 [hereinafter the Jan. 2015 Order]. First Mercury then drafted a Notice of Entry of Orders, Jan. 13, 2015 [hereinafter Notice] and served the Notice and Jan. 2015 Order on January 13, 2015. Bornstein denies that the Orders were in fact served, but fails to attest to that fact in declaration. By contrast, Lorna Walker attests to service of the Notice and the Jan. 2015 Order in declaration. And both Parties attach to their respective declarations what they claim to be true and correct copies of the Jan. 2015 Order and the Notice of Entry of Orders-both of which include a proof of service stating the documents were served via e-mail on January 13, 2015. Thus all admissible evidence (and zero countervailing admissible evidence) indicates that the Notice and the Jan. 2015 Order attached thereto were in fact served on Bornstein. (see part 2 and 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK HOOSHMAND VS. KATHARINA WOHL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Mark Hooshmand'S Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl (Part 2 Of 2 For Posting Of Tentative Ruling)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday May 1, 2015, Line 5, Plaintiff Mark Hooshmand'S Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl (Part 2 Of 3 For Posting Of Tentative Ruling). To complicate matters, however, First Mercury failed to actually file the Notice and the attached Jan. 2015 Order with the Court. The Clerk of Court does have the Jan. 2015 Order filed on February 4, 2015. But the Notice of having served the Jan. 2015 Order is conspicuously absent. Bornstein argues the failure to file the Notice divests this Court of jurisdiction to enforce the Jan. 2015 Order via the instant Motion. After a search of every case that cites to Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1019.5, no authority can be found on point. Nor does Bornstein cite to any authority. First Mercury is of no help, having declined to file a Reply. Thus the Court engages in statutory construction. "The statutory language is the best indicator of legislative intent. Indeed, the most powerful safeguard for the courts' adherence to their constitutional role of construing, rather than writing, statutes is to rely on the statute's plain language." Med. Bd. of California v. Superior Court, 88 Cal. App. 4th 1001, 1014 (2001) (alterations and internal quotation marks omitted). Here, the statute simply requires "notice of the court's decision." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 1019.5(a). The statute does not require filing of the notice. The evidence indicates the Jan. 2015 Order was properly served on January 13, 2015. Moreover, First Mercury issued a letter to Bornstein on February 18, 2015 notifying Bornstein again of the Jan. 2015 Order and meeting and conferring as to the Instant Motion. Again, Bornstein fails to deny this letter either in argument or in declaration. The totality of the admissible evidence indicates that Bornstein had constructive notice of the Jan. 2015 Order, and very likely had actual notice as well. Under these facts, section 1019.5 is satisfied. The Motion for Terminating Sanctions is DENIED. The Motion for Issue Sanctions and/or Evidentiary Sanctions is GRANTED. First Mercury shall prepare a proposed order seeking issue sanctions as they relate to the unanswered interrogatories, and evidence sanctions as they relate to the documents sought but not produced under the Jan. 2015 Order. (see part 1 and 3) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARK HOOSHMAND VS. KATHARINA WOHL et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiff Mark Hooshmand'S Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl (Part 3 Of 3 For Posting Of Tentative Ruling).
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Friday May 1, 2015, Line 5, Plaintiff Mark Hooshmand'S Motion To Compel Discovery Responses From Defendant Katharina Wohl; Request For Monetary Sanctions As To Defendant Katharina Wohl And Defense Counsel Christopher Wohl (Part 3 Of 3 For Posting Of Tentative Ruling). The Code mandates: "The court shall impose a monetary . . . against any party, person, or attorney who unsuccessfully . . . opposes a motion to compel further response, unless it finds that the one subject to the sanction acted with substantial justification or that other circumstances make the imposition of the sanction unjust." Cal. Civ. Proc. Code § 2033.290(d). The Court can find no especially compelling reason as to why Bornstein has habitually ignored lawfully issued discovery or orders of this Court. Bornstein's excuse that he was not served notice of the Jan. 2015 Order falls flat. Borstein and/or his counsel were at the January 2015 hearing. He was aware of the impending orders. He was served notice of the Jan. 2015 Order. Still, four months later, well aware of the existence of the Jan. 2015 Order, Bornstein still openly defies it, failing to respond to discovery. The Court can find no "substantial justification" for Bornstein's opposition to the Instant Motion. Monetary sanctions in the amount of $650 is GRANTED. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to drobbins@woodrobbins.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. (see part 1 and 2) =(302/JPT)
